title: Undo the Equation
module: Week 1 - Linear Equations, Inequalities, and Functions
learningTarget: I can solve one-step and two-step equations and explain the inverse operation I used.
whyItMatters: CAASPP questions often hide simple equation solving inside a word problem, table, or graph. If you can undo an equation cleanly, you can keep the rest of the problem from turning into noise.

vocabulary.definition: A math sentence that says two expressions are equal.
A letter that stands for an unknown number.
The number multiplying a variable. In 5x, the coefficient is 5.
An operation that undoes another operation, like subtraction undoing addition.
Get the variable by itself on one side of the equation.
Put your answer back into the original equation to see if it makes the equation true.

sections.title: Overview Reflection
sections.studentText: After watching the overview, explain the main point in your own words.

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain the main idea of the video. How is deconstructing an equation similar to untying a knot?
questions.feedback: A strong response explains that solving means undoing operations step by step while keeping both sides balanced until the variable is isolated.

sections.title: Quick Start
sections.studentText: Before solving, check the main balance rule. An equation only stays true when both sides are treated the same way.

questions.prompt: If you subtract 2 from the left side of an equation, what should you do to keep it balanced?

options.text: Subtract 2 from the right side too

questions.feedback: Whatever you do to one side, you do to the other side. That keeps the equation balanced.

sections.title: Watch: Level one linear equations
sections.watchFor: Watch how the teacher turns 5x = 20 into a question you can answer, then shows the systematic divide-by-the-coefficient method.

contextQuestions.prompt: In the video, what does 5x mean?
contextQuestions.feedback: The video explains that a number written next to a variable means multiplication, so 5x means 5 times x.

contextQuestions.prompt: For 5x = 20, what operation does the teacher use to isolate x?
contextQuestions.feedback: The coefficient is 5, so divide both sides by 5 to get x = 4.

contextQuestions.prompt: The video says the coefficient is the number being multiplied by x. In -5/6x = 7/8, what is the coefficient?
contextQuestions.promptHtml: The video says the coefficient is the number being multiplied by x. In -56x = 78, what is the coefficient?
expectedElements: negative five sixths is multiplying x
contextQuestions.feedback: The coefficient is -5/6 because that is the number multiplying x.

contextQuestions.prompt: Why does the teacher check the answer by substituting it back into the original equation?
contextQuestions.feedback: A strong answer says checking proves the solution makes the original equation true.

sections.title: The Equation Undo Button
sections.studentText: Solving an equation means finding the value that makes the sentence true. The main move is to undo operations in reverse order while keeping both sides balanced.

workedExample.commonMistake: A common mistake is subtracting 7 from only one side. That changes the equation instead of solving it.

sections.title: Balance Lab: Move the Same Way on Both Sides
sections.studentText: Use the undo board to solve 3x + 2 = 11. Drag the highlighted part into the undo box, then watch what has to happen to both sides.

steps.action: Remove two 1 tiles from both sides
steps.explanation: The two +1 tiles are gone from the left, and two 1 tiles are removed from the right. That leaves 3x = 9.
Three x groups share 9 equally, so each x is 3.

balanceLab.complete: You kept the equation balanced: x = 3.

sections.title: Real-World Equation: Club Merch Drop
sections.studentText: This is a step up from the warm-up. A school club is selling two kinds of merch at lunch. Stickers are $3 each, buttons are $2 each, and the club already has $7 in the cash box. If they sell the same number of stickers and buttons and end with $47, you can model the situation with 3x + 2x + 7 = 47.

image.alt: Student selling sticker packs and button packs at a school lunch table with a 3x + 2x + 7 = 47 equation on a board.
image.caption: The picture shows the same situation as the equation: sticker packs, button packs, $7 already in the cash box, and a $47 total.

questions.prompt: Before solving, combine the variable terms in 3x + 2x + 7 = 47. What does 3x + 2x become, and why?
commonMisconceptions: cannot combine because the prices are different
questions.feedback: Because both terms have x, 3x + 2x combines to 5x.

questions.prompt: Solve the simplified equation 5x + 7 = 47. What is x?
questions.feedback: Subtract 7 to get 5x = 40, then divide by 5. x = 8.

questions.prompt: Explain what x = 8 means in the merch situation.
expectedElements: same number of stickers and buttons
questions.feedback: x represents the number of each item, so the club sold 8 stickers and 8 buttons.
Add 6 to both sides, then divide by 5.

questions.prompt: A student solves 2x + 9 = 21 by writing 2x = 30, so x = 15. What went wrong?
questions.feedback: The student added 9 instead of subtracting 9. The correct first step is 2x = 12.
The equation has -10, so add 10 to undo it.

questions.prompt: Explain why checking your solution is worth the extra ten seconds.
questions.feedback: A strong answer says checking proves the value makes the original equation true.

gate.completion: Student attempts the video questions, practice, and exit check.
gate.mastery: At least 70% on the exit check, with practice available for revision until the skill is solid.
gate.remediation: Review inverse operations and retry two equations with new numbers.
gate.enrichment: Write a believable wrong solution and explain the exact mistake.
